claim: recall-at-decision-moments guidance should gate firing on expected value to avoid over-fire
recall phase timing (~350s label) and whether 'faster' is structurally hard for memory ops
recall payload is not the cost bottleneck — NEWER confirms this and extends it: cost, speed, and compounding are all refuted as memory levers; only idiosyncratic capability survives

how to handle 'buried at scale' conditions in evals — OLDER says use a stub binary; NEWER refines: first run a free retrieval probe; only use a stub when the probe confirms the stressor cannot surface naturally
vague 'reconcile before recommending / do not displace' instruction is insufficient — must also name the rationalization pattern to halve residual failures
engram recall merges crystallized notes and raw chunks into one unified cosine ranking with no separate retrieval path
CLI commands taking path arguments must be verified with the real binary and real paths, not just unit tests
do not retry a bash command that returned permission denied — the failure is deterministic
do not remove t.Parallel() to fix a flaky parallel test — fix the shared mutable state instead
Go PBT tool roles: rapid for explicit-oracle property tests, go test -fuzz for no-crash oracle, rapid.MakeFuzz as the bridge
recall-only duration (~190s) — NEWER shows recall_s timer actually includes the full first build, so the ~190s figure cited in OLDER requires reinterpretation of what was measured
engram memory's cost/speed payoff — OLDER says both cheaper and faster are achievable; NEWER narrows: cost and speed wins are refuted/below noise; only idiosyncratic capability survives
whether recall over-firing is a problem worth fixing — OLDER says glance relaxes the ceiling; NEWER says Joe rejected fixing over-fire as not a concern at all
whether raw chunks and vault notes travel separate retrieval paths — OLDER says they compete and chunks surface poorly; NEWER says they merge into one unified cosine ranking with no separate path
reducing per-fire cost is the lever to increase usage — NEWER narrows: a glance rung reduces per-fire cost but does NOT dissolve the over-fire ceiling or the separate value gate
whether over-firing recall is a meaningful cost/risk to gate against
the over-fire tax formula (tax = over-fire x per-fire-cost) applies to recall depth variants — glance lowers per-fire-cost but does not dissolve the ceiling or value gate
whether to split an agent skill across models to cut cost
whether engram recall uses separate retrieval paths for notes vs chunks
whether memory-assisted builds can be made cheaper than cold builds
whether raw chunks compete in the same ranking space as crystallized vault notes
whether engram recall uses separate retrieval paths/tiers for notes vs chunks
C5 (honor-standard) is a verified capability win for engram memory — NEWER shows glance rung FAILS C5, narrowing full-depth recall's C5 win to deep (not glance) recall
whether a worded value gate can hold the over-fire ceiling — OLDER says the gate remains necessary and operative; NEWER says the value gate did not hold via wording on opus-4.8 regardless of prose

using homogeneous-age distractors in a recency eval causes no discrimination — OLDER attributed this to band guarantee masking half-life, NEWER generalizes it to any age-uniform distractor pool masking all decay parameter differences
